sentences.text: After I was taken back from the village, from Buly.
On the occasion of-- of Christmas, I wrote them a thank you letter.
Remember, I had only one year of schooling by that time, so I was not exactly Shakespeare.
I forgot about the letter.
I wrote the letter.
I forgot about it.
We went back-- '94 I believe, was it? '95?
I want to go back to chase the demons, so to speak.
Immediately after the communist regime fell-- I couldn't go before that, because I was a member of the Israeli Ministry of Defense establishment for many years.
And I didn't dare to go to any communist held country.
I would not come back.
So I waited.
It didn't even cross my mind to go while the communists were there.
And when they disappeared, I toyed with the idea.
Eventually somebody went and came back and said-- a member of my family said-- she said the hatred is so thick that-- that she was appalled.
And I couldn't take-- I couldn't make myself go back after that statement.
So I delayed it.
Eventually, I went back.
And I went back to Buly, found it on the map, found-- found all the places.
I told you already, found it on the map-- knew exactly where the turnoff will be-- told my wife before that here will be the turnoff.
Couldn't find the place, couldn't find-- couldn't find the bridge where the massacre happened.
Not the first time.
The old road, which was at the bottom of the valley, was not there anymore.
It was a new modern road on the side of the-- of the mountain-- not-- not the old road.
You could see part of the old road, but not-- I Couldn't find it-- not the first time-- I couldn't find the-- I couldn't find the bridge.
But anyway I found it, that place.
I found this old lady Maria Baluhova, who I already told you about.
She gave me the name of the people.
I never recollected the name of the people.
She gave me the name of the people.
I left her my card, my visit card-- my business card.
I left more than one business card.
I also left some little presents, some money.
She was so kind, so nice.
She was so warm.
She was so human.
She was so different from the hatred not only that I recalled, but that I found in Slovakia when I came this time.
You could-- you could cut the hatred.
It was so thick.
The stories, the encounters now in the '90s with people in the street was just unbelievable.
Anyway, she was such a fresh breath of-- of fresh air, of humanity, of-- of-- of-- of kindness.
So she was there with her-- with her grandson who is an engineer by the name of Jaroslav [?
Langoure. ?] I just corresponded with him, and we are now in touch.
Jaroslav lived in [NON-ENGLISH].
And Anna-- so sorry, I didn't come to Anna.
After the war, the Striharceks had a daughter by the name of Anna.
They died in the '70s.
I always regretted that I didn't go.
Now that I found out that they've died in the '70s, I didn't regret anymore.
There was no way for me to be there in the '70s.
It was under the communist era, so the fact that I didn't go immediately didn't change it.
I-- I-- I felt that I should have gone immediately, but--
So Maria Baluhova and her-- her-- her grandson, Jaroslav, told me that-- that Anna was their daughter.
She married.
She married a guy by the name of Igor Ivanovich-- Ivanich.
Ivanich.
Ivanich, Igor Ivanich.
And now her name is Anna Ivanovichova.
Igor Ivanich and Anna Ivanovichova.
They are devout Catholics, very devout Catholics.
And they visit their ancestral home and they rent it out, make up some money this way.
And many times they rent it out to priests free because there are so many-- so devout.
And I became very hesitant, but I gave the card-- gave him a card, and gave him additional cards to give to Anna and tell Anna about me, and left it at that.
And my-- my attitude was that I will not write.
If-- if she wants a contact with a Jew, she will start making contact with a Jew.
If she doesn't, then I never met her.
She never me me.
We got the most wonderful letter from her.
Absolutely unbelievable.
She said that she heard about me a lot, that-- that her parents told her that they intended to keep me if my parents will not come, and that they loved me very, much.
And they kept my letter.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] They gave the letter to her.
And she kept it all these years.
She married.
She has two children, Maria and Miro, a-- a daughter and a-- and a son.
I don't know in which order, son or daughter.
But Maria-- and we have pictures of-- of them.
She sent pictures, not in the first letter, in the second letter after I replied.
And she kept all these years the letter.
And I told you before that there's kind of supporting evidence to my approach.
I think this-- this is it.
Eventually I asked her to send me a copy of the letter, which she did.
And to my recent birthday, Dahlia, my wife, took the letter, her picture and an embroidery that Anna made and sent us-- a simple embroidery, but made by her.
She does it.
They are-- they are retired.
And they have to be very young, possibly retired not by choice.
He-- He's not very healthy.
He has a heart condition, they're retired and live on pension, which is quite modest.
So they have a little land that they cultivate for their own need.
And they rent out this-- this place, and they-- and she-- she embroiders and sells embroidery.
So she sends the embroidery.
So we framed all of it, and now I have it upstairs.
I just wrote her a letter and sent her something small for her sending us somethings small for-- for Christmas.
But this is the only light in that episode.
So hopefully we'll go back and see her.
I cannot afford to bring all of them here.
But hopefully we'll go back and see her.
See, I've never seen her in person.
Picture and letter not frequently because Slovak is difficult and about once, twice a year.
But we keep in touch.
So eventually my father took me back.
Well, how long did you stay all together in Buly?
In that--
Approximately seven month, from the end of October, November, December till-- till end of May-- seven months.
Well, I was taken away, and then we wrote the letter.
And then events took over.
My-- my father died, and I left Slovakia.
Yes.
In which sense?
I don't think so.
I-- I really-- very, very many time, when I think of dramatic depictions of emotions of meetings-- I wonder how-- how true they are.
I think everybody in that period was on emotional overload.
I don't think there were any emotions left.
I think you take life-- took life one day at a time.
I don't think I had any emotion when these people were shot just behind the house.
I don't think I had any emotional.
I think I grieve them today more.
[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] I don't think I-- I cried for them then.
People were dead emotionally.
I-- I just don't believe these dramatizations.
We took things-- you know, you are alive, you are alive.
You are dead, you are dead.
I was brought back.
I was brought back.
It's great.
Here is here.
No.
No, it was not my father.
Someone-- somebody came and brought me in.
It was not my father.
And we didn't go back to-- to Novo Mesto.
I was not brought back to Novo Mesto.
My father was in Bratislava, which was the capital city of Slovakia-- of Slovakia.
But at that time, it was not Slovakia any more.
It was already Czechoslovakia.
That was the main city in Slovakia, and we lived in Slovakia.
I-- I-- I don't remember moving very much.
We lived in a place in Slovakia.
Again, I have pictures of the house.
And I even know the street.
It was called [?
Boshena ?] [?
Nimsva ?] number 6.
And I was brought there.
My father eventually remarried, and my stepmother brought me to Israel after he died.
Her name was [?
Yolan ?] [?
Benau. ?] And obviously it changes to [?
Yolan ?] Frieder later.
And now she goes under the name Ilana.
She still lives.
She's quite old.
She lives in Israel.
So he remarried-- not in a public ceremony-- in a private ceremony.
And then we-- the three of us lived in that place.
I was put back in school.
I skipped second grade, I-- and I was put straight in third grade.
I-- And this was a Jewish school?
No, it was a regular--
--public school.
And in the afternoon I was supposed-- I say supposed-- I was going to a Cheder, which is a Jewish supplemental school.
But most of the time I skipped it.
I-- I was given the money to go on the subway.
I would go-- not subway, the-- the tram, the electric car.
I would walk from our house down to the electric car and go to the Jewish-- Jewish quarter where the Cheder was.
But most of the time I would look at the Danube.
Most of the time I would skip it.
My father was busy.
My mother didn't care-- my stepmother didn't care.
I didn't care.
I was doing well in school, in regular school.
I-- they may have been concerned,
No, about not going to the Cheder.
But I read Hebrew fluently.
I-- I was no problem.
I was a voracious reader in Slovak.
I knew all my prayers.
I knew everything.
The house was very Jewish.
They may not have been too much concerned.
Maybe they were.
Yes, he became the chief rabbi of Slovakia.
And he became the head of the-- of the [NON-ENGLISH], of the organization of the Jewish congregations.

That is the time that I discovered the first time the difference between super Hasidic Judaism and the type of Judaism we subscribed to, which was the regular-- regular-- regular Judaism, not Hasidic.
It was not conservative in the American sense.
It was orthodox in the American sense-- even very orthodox in the American sense, but not orthodox in the-- in the-- in the European sense.
I know that because they gave him a lot of trouble.
And then I recalled that even before the war, before-- before the German invasion, he was referring to them as the 45's. 45 was the caliber of a big gun.
So they-- they gave him a lot of trouble because they were not Zionist.
And there was a clash between the Zionist rabbis and Zionist movement and the strict orthodoxy, the Hasidic orthodoxy.
It was it was a-- it was an ideological clash, and one which continues still today.
He was very active in-- in-- in the Zionist movement.
He was very active in the Jewish life.
He tried to resurrect the Jewish life.
He was very active in the political life of the country, but he died very quickly after that.
With the exception of the low countries that we called-- they called it low.
I mean, not low-- not low culturally, low physically, Denmark, Holland, Belgium.
With the exception of those countries and then some of the Scandinavians, obviously.
Scandinavians.
Europe has nothing to be proud of for their conduct in the Second World War, relative to the Jews.
And with the current revelations about the information which is available today lies I think the dark part of the history of mankind.
Yes, very normal.
I-- I recall going to the circus.
I can actually recall going to movies, I recall going to school, sliding down the side of the mountain in the winter.
And, yes, it was-- it was very normal.
There had to be, because I remember sliding down with-- slides with children and being chastised very much by the adults.
Because when you slide down at all, it becomes more icy.
And if it becomes more icy, then it's-- it's bad for the adults.
Children could care less-- slide on their bottom, right?
No.
No.
It was the first time.
I-- I had to be in Bratislava before, because my maternal grandparents lived in Bratislava before they came to Novo Mesto.
They had a house there.
So I have been there visiting them.
And I recall the big house.
My grandmother-- maternal grandmother survived.
There were many members of-- on my mother's side, her brothers and sisters, which left in the '30s, which decided this is not a place for Jews to be, and left in the '30s.
They went to Argentina.
They went to-- to England, to-- to Palestine.
They-- they left the country, and they survived.
Most of them died within the last two years, three years.
So they-- they enjoyed a long, lengthy, fruitful life.
I still have from my mother's side-- one sister lives now in Jerusalem.
She was in the British army during the Second World War.
Surgery.
Complication after surgery or from neglect.
There were rumors after the war, after he died, that he was killed on purpose-- on-- surgically killed, so to speak.
There-- there is no support for that story-- not that he didn't have enemies.
And obviously lots of the collaborators of Slovakia which were still alive and knew they were documented would like him to see dead.
But there's no-- no support for that.
The person who-- who was the-- the surgeon was one of the more respected surgeons somebody knew.
And so many respected him.
He just died, because in 1946, surgery was not as advanced as today.
And medicine and life support was not as available as today.
And people died.
Also his physique was very much weakened by the Second World War.
Oh yes, I did.
I told him the story, and he wrote the story down in his memoirs.
No.
I-- I don't recall that I was emotional about it all.
I-- I don't recall that I was emotional about that period at all until I became a teenager.
When I was a teenager, I would wake up at night crying.
It's-- it's like a delayed action.
I do not recall any emotions throughout the World War.
I was-- I was hit.
I was endangered.
I was surviving like a rat between mice.
I knew what to do to survive.
I knew what to say, not to say.
I was not emotional.
I think the emotions hit once-- once the tension went away, once I was free.

I think the emotion hits once I came to-- to-- to Israel, and suddenly was surrounded by people who didn't know what I went through, and for whom I was a bizarre remnant of an era they did not understand.
And they did not go through the danger.
And-- and suddenly the-- the-- the difference for what could have been and what was hit me.
And-- and all the emotions came through.
I would-- I would wake up at night crying, almost every night for-- for years.
In Israel-- and kind of mourning.
One year.
Enough to arrange a transfer to Israel on a illegal--
No, I was-- I was--
I was very young.
I was-- I was 10 years old, hardly-- I was not 10 years old when we came to Israel.
My father wanted her to do that.
He told her, don't stay in this bloody-- this land full of blood.
I don't know.
He may have.
I don't know.
But his views about that place were very clear.
It's a place of blood, and crime, and hatred.
Still today, with the exception of people like Anna and Maria Baluhova, which I think are-- are the minority.
These are people from the very, very small villages.
This-- this-- this-- the people in the village in Buly, I don't know if they knew what money is.
It was all like barter.
Barter economy, where they swapped shoes.
I told you I was-- I was helping this guy to make the-- the-- the thread for the pelt shoes, and he was swapping the shoes for other things.
I don't think it was the money based community.
Those people were humans.
Others just hated.
Yes.
What-- what happened is Palestine-- it was not-- it was before the structure, before they started.
It was the mandate.
It was under the mandate of United Nations.
It was a mandate of the pre-United Nations.
The-- I don't know how it's called-- how the-- that organization was called.
After the Second World War, there was an organization, how it was called-- it was not called United Nations.
It was-- I know in Hebrew it's called differently than the United Nations.
In Hebrew, it's called [HEBREW], which is the Organization of Nations, or-- or the Brotherhood of Nations.
I don't know what the official English name of that organization was.
British were given mandate over that part of the world, which continued throughout the war.
So the British were in control.
The British would not let Jews come in, except in limited numbers.
The [HEBREW], the-- the Jewish military organization of the [HEBREW] of the Jewish agency, organized illegal entry.
And we were organized such illegal entry in the most intriguing way.
This is a story I was told.
I never checked its accuracy, but I have no reason to doubt it.
And if the details are not exact, the-- the gist is exact, because it happened.
There was a British-- I'm sorry.
There was a Czechoslovak brigade fighting under the British army in the Second World War.
That is a fact.
After the war, somebody had the idea that all these soldiers would visit the Holy Land.
They got permission to do so.
The [HEBREW] bought all these permissions, structured families according to the permissions.
And one day we-- we were put on a train and went to Prague, stayed a couple of days in Prague, continued with the train to Marseille, France.
In Marseilles, we boarded a French ship called The French ship sailed through the Mediterranean to Alexandria and then docked in Haifa, where everybody went down, but nobody came back.
So there was an illegal legal-illegal [HEBREW].
Somebody paid a lot of money, because all these people, all these tourists brought furniture with them.
So-- but anyway, that's how we got there in 1947.
I was part of that group.
My mother got a-- some kind of husband, which was too young to have me as a-- as a child.
But as I said, it was just pro forma.
June.
June.
June, 1947.
As a family, just the two of us.
